questions.question: Describe the structure of a block in a blockchain. List and explain all the fields in the block header.
keyPoints: Two main parts: block header and block body (transactions).
Header fields: version, previous block hash, Merkle root, timestamp, difficulty target, nonce.
Previous block hash links blocks together forming the chain.
Merkle root summarizes all transactions in a single hash.

questions.question: Explain how the block structure ensures immutability in a blockchain. Describe the role of the previous block hash and Merkle root in maintaining data integrity.
keyPoints: Each block header contains the hash of the previous block header.
Changing any transaction changes the Merkle root hash.
Changed Merkle root changes the block header hash.
Changed block hash mismatches the next block's previous hash field.
This cascading effect invalidates all subsequent blocks.
An attacker would need to re-mine all subsequent blocks, which is computationally infeasible.

questions.question: What is the Merkle root and how is it computed from the transactions in a block?
keyPoints: Merkle root is the root hash of a binary hash tree (Merkle tree).
Pairs of hashes are concatenated and hashed together.
Process repeats until a single root hash remains.
Enables efficient verification (SPV) without downloading all transactions.

questions.question: Explain the role of the nonce and difficulty target in the block structure with respect to Proof-of-Work mining.
keyPoints: Nonce is a 32-bit value miners adjust to change the block header hash.
Difficulty target is the threshold the hash must be below.
Miners increment the nonce and rehash until hash < target.
Difficulty is adjusted periodically to maintain consistent block creation rate.
